worth_it_explanation: Names are the handles a publisher uses to slice reporting, target deals, and find the right line in a list that runs to hundreds of entries. When APS tag and placement names follow a parseable convention, a report can break revenue out by page type, position, or device without guessing which name means what. When deal and package names are consistent, the right buyer reaches the right inventory and reconciliation against the demand side lines up. The check reads names a publisher already has, so it costs review time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete renaming the publisher can apply.
intro: Amazon Publisher Services (APS) tags, placements, deals, and packages all carry names, and those names are how a publisher reads its own setup later. A name that encodes the page type, position, size, and device lets reporting be sliced along the lines that matter, while a name that is a one-off label or an auto-generated string forces guesswork every time someone reads a report. This subcategory is about the naming discipline itself rather than the auction or pricing behind it. These checks confirm that APS names follow a consistent, parseable convention and that deal and package names are identifiable enough to target and reconcile.

audit_checks.title: Tag and placement names do not follow a consistent, parseable convention
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Inconsistent placement names do not break the auction, so the cost is indirect. Reporting cannot be sliced cleanly by page type, position, or device, which slows analysis and hides which inventory earns. The risk grows with the size of the setup because a list of one-off names becomes harder to read as placements multiply.

educate.base:
Amazon Publisher Services (APS) assigns an identifier to each ad slot a publisher sends into Transparent Ad Marketplace or Unified Ad Marketplace, and the publisher chooses the label that sits alongside it. That label is what shows up in reporting and in the slot configuration, so it is the handle a person uses to tell one placement from another. When the label encodes the facts that matter, the page type, the position on the page, the size, and the device, a report can be broken out along any of those lines. When the label is a one-off string such as 'div-1' or an auto-generated identifier, the only way to read it is to remember what it meant when it was created.

A parseable convention is a fixed order of fields joined by a separator, so a name can be split back into its parts by eye or by a script. A common shape is page type, then position, then size, then device, for example 'article_atf_300x250_mobile'. The exact fields are the publisher's choice, but the discipline is that every name uses the same fields in the same order with the same separator. Header-bidding practice treats this as a reporting and deal-targeting convention rather than a cosmetic one because a stable, descriptive slot label is what lets demand be matched and revenue be sliced at the placement level. Prebid documents the same idea through the Global Placement ID (GPID), a stable identifier for each slot that keeps duplicate slots distinct and improves how each placement is recognized.

The cost of inconsistent names is paid every time someone reads a report. A publisher cannot ask which page types earn the most, or whether above-the-fold inventory clears higher than below-the-fold, if the names do not carry page type or position in a form a filter can read. Two placements that mean the same thing but are named differently split their data, and one placement reused across pages hides the difference between them. None of this shows up as an error, so it persists until someone tries to analyze the inventory and finds the names will not support the question.

A few signs point to placement names that are not parseable:
  • Names mix conventions, with some encoding page type and size and others left as 'ad1' or a div ID
  • The same placement appears under more than one name, or one name covers placements that should be told apart
  • Reporting cannot be filtered by page type, position, or device because those facts are not in the names in a consistent form
You may want to confirm that APS placement names follow one convention that carries the fields the publisher reports on. One approach is to write down the convention, list the current names against it, and rename the placements that do not fit.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the placements that matter most for analysis are usually section fronts, article bodies, and the homepage, each at a known position. You may want a convention that carries page type and position so a report can separate a homepage above-the-fold unit from an article-footer unit because those clear at different prices and a name that hides the difference hides the revenue story.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages sit alongside editorial pages and tend to reach a higher-intent audience. You may want the page-type field in the convention to distinguish commercial pages from editorial ones, so reporting can show whether the commercial placements earn the premium their audience suggests rather than blending into an editorial average.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Ad Slot and GPID for Header Bidding
Ad Unit Reference for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed configuration documentation for tag and placement setup is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the names assigned to APS tags and placements and check them against a single stated convention, looking for names that do not parse into the same fields.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the Amazon Publisher Services console and list the tags or placements configured for Transparent Ad Marketplace and Unified Ad Marketplace.
Record the name of each placement and note which fields it encodes, for example page type, position, size, or device.
Compare the names against one convention and flag any that mix fields, omit a field the others carry, or use a one-off label such as a div ID.
Note any case where the same placement appears under more than one name, or one name is reused across placements that should be told apart.

reporting_review.label: Reporting review
steps: Open APS reporting and try to break revenue out by page type, position, and device using the placement names.
Record whether the names support those breakouts cleanly or whether the slice fails because the facts are not carried consistently in the names.
Note the specific dimensions that cannot be reported on because the naming convention does not encode them.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each APS tag and placement name and whether it parses into the same fields in the same order as the stated convention. A setup where every name follows one convention that carries the fields the publisher reports on passes. A setup where names mix conventions, reuse a name across distinct placements, or omit fields needed for reporting is a fail recorded with the names that do not fit and the breakouts they block.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the APS placement names and the convention they should follow above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Amazon Publisher Services (APS) tag and placement names on {{site_name}} do not follow one consistent, parseable convention, so reporting cannot be sliced cleanly by page type, position, or device. A descriptive, stable slot label is what lets revenue be broken out at the placement level and demand be matched to the right inventory, and names left as one-off labels or auto-generated strings cannot support those questions. {{voice_recommend}} writing down a convention that carries the fields {{site_name}} reports on, such as page type, position, size, and device in a fixed order, then renaming the placements that do not fit. After the change, confirm that reporting can be filtered by each of those fields.

audit_checks.title: Deal and package names are inconsistent or not identifiable
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal that cannot be identified by its name is hard to target to the right buyer and hard to reconcile against the demand side, which can leave a deal underdelivering or misattributed without an error to flag it. Because deals carry negotiated pricing and named buyers, a naming gap here touches revenue more directly than a placement label does.

educate.base:
Amazon Publisher Services (APS) lets a publisher package inventory into deals and curated packages that a named buyer can transact against, including curation through Amazon Publisher Cloud. Each deal and package carries a name, and that name is how both sides find the same line. The publisher uses it to target the right inventory and read delivery in reporting, and the buyer uses it to confirm the deal they negotiated is the deal they are buying. A name that identifies the buyer, the inventory, and the deal type makes that match plain. A name that is a stray label or a duplicated string forces both sides to confirm by other means.

Identity matters more for a deal than for a placement because a deal carries a negotiated price and a named counterparty. A deal whose name does not say who it is for, or whose name repeats across two different agreements, is hard to target to the right buyer and hard to reconcile when the publisher's delivery numbers are compared against the buyer's. The reconciliation step is where deal names earn their keep because matching a publisher-side deal to a demand-side deal depends on both naming it in a form the other can recognize.

The IAB Tech Lab sellers.json standard sits alongside this. It requires that a seller identifier map to a single named legal entity that is paid for the inventory, so the seller a buyer sees is unambiguous. A publisher's own deal naming should follow the same spirit, where one name means one deal with one buyer, so the name a buyer sees in a bid request or a deal list resolves to exactly one agreement.

A few signs point to deal and package names that are not identifiable:
  • Deal names do not carry the buyer, the inventory, or the deal type, so the name alone does not say what the deal is
  • The same name appears on more than one deal, or two names describe what is meant to be the same deal
  • Reconciliation against the demand side relies on deal IDs passed by hand because the names do not line up
You may want to confirm that each APS deal and package name identifies its buyer and inventory and is unique. One approach is to adopt a deal-naming convention, for example buyer, then inventory, then deal type, and rename deals that do not carry those fields. Header-bidding practice uses a comparable shape for line items, naming them by format, bidder, and price so the line is identifiable at a glance.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, deals often sell a section or a content theme to a particular advertiser, so the inventory and the buyer are both worth carrying in the name. You may want the convention to name the section or theme alongside the buyer, so a report can show how a sponsorship of one section delivered without anyone tracing it back through a deal ID.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals may target product or category pages for endemic advertisers whose products match the page. You may want the deal name to carry the category and the buyer, so the publisher can reconcile a category-targeted deal against the demand side and confirm the endemic advertiser reached the inventory it paid for.

learn_more.source_title: Google Ad Manager with Prebid Step by Step (line item naming)

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed documentation for configuring deals and curated packages, including Amazon Publisher Cloud, is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the names on APS deals and curated packages and check whether each one identifies its buyer and inventory and is unique across the setup.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the Amazon Publisher Services console and list the active deals and curated packages, including any built through Amazon Publisher Cloud.
Record each deal name and note whether it carries the buyer, the inventory it targets, and the deal type.
Flag any deal name that does not identify its buyer or inventory, and any name that appears on more than one deal.
Note whether two names describe what is meant to be the same deal, which splits its reporting.

reconciliation_review.label: Reconciliation review
steps: Take a sample of deals and try to match each publisher-side deal name to its counterpart on the demand side using the names alone.
Record whether the names line up or whether the match relies on deal IDs passed by hand because the names do not correspond.
Note any deal where delivery cannot be confirmed against the buyer because the name does not resolve to a single agreement.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each APS deal and package name and whether it identifies the buyer and inventory and is unique. A setup where every deal name carries those fields and resolves to a single agreement passes. A setup where deal names omit the buyer or inventory, repeat across distinct deals, or fail to line up with the demand side for reconciliation is a fail recorded with the deals affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the APS deal and package names and whether each identifies its buyer and inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Amazon Publisher Services (APS) deal and package names on {{site_name}} do not consistently identify the buyer and inventory, and some names repeat across distinct deals. A deal carries a negotiated price and a named buyer, so a name that does not resolve to a single agreement is hard to target and hard to reconcile against the demand side, which can leave a deal underdelivering or misattributed without an error to flag it. {{voice_recommend}} adopting a deal-naming convention that carries the buyer, the inventory, and the deal type, then renaming the deals that do not fit and resolving any duplicate names. After the change, confirm that each deal name lines up with its demand-side counterpart for reconciliation.
